text.fullText: From: David <ddow420@comcast.net> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/11/2026 12:33:12 PM Subject: Public Comment on Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-2025-229 -------------------------------------------------------- You don't often get email from ddow420@comcast.net. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Sir/Madam: When I worked at NOAA Fisheries Northeast Fisheries Science Center Woods Hole Laboratory, I participated in the Waquoit Bay Watershed Ecological Risk Assessment project which focused on stressors (Climate change; nutrient pollution; overfishing and toxic chemical contamination) on wild places, wild things. In modern times one would add environmental justice and affordable/attainable housing as socioeconomic factors in this Cape Cod watershed where my church is located. I spent over 40 years as an environmental activist at the Superfund/Safe Drinking Water Act cleanup at Joint Base Cape Cod which has expended $1.3 billion on the cleanup of groundwater/protection of of the Upper Cape Cod Water Supply Reserve (our future drinking water supply). The fish and shellfish in local rivers/ponds is contaminated with PFAS chemicals at levels of concern for Mashpee Wampanoag Tribal members and freshwater anglers seeking food to put food on the table (based on monitoring by University of Rhode Island Sources, Transport, Exposure and Effects of PFAS). I used to be the Recreational Fisheries Coordinator in the Northeast. JBCC is on state land and they utilize the Massa. Department of Environmental Protection PFAS 6 (20 parts per trillion) as the maximum contaminant level for the SDWA cleanup (Which is still in process for PFAS contaminants). Governor Healy turned down Congressionally allocated funds for the Massa. Army National Guard Multipurpose Machine Gun Range because off the slow progress of the Air Force Civil Engineering Center SDWA/CERCLA cleanup. She is awaiting the US EPA Regon 1 report on the PFAS cleanup The proposed US EPA for section 401 of the Clean Water Act which limits state and indigenous government review of Clean Water Act projects is likely to generate similar opposition and delays in various clean water projects and cleanups due to law suits/Congressional legislation. Thus I urge US EPA to: I urge the EPA to reconsider its recent update of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act limiting the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and ultimately, clean, drinking water. Federal and state cooperation is foundational to America's system of government, and Section 401 has been critical to putting the public's interest above the wants of polluters. After recent rollbacks of federal clean water protections, state, tribal and local input has become even more necessary. The EPA's proposed rule weakening Section 401 undermines the will of Americans who want clean water protections and the ability of states and tribes to review and possibly block projects that may threaten public health and degrade the waterways and wetlands within their borders. I urge the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401. Sincerely, Dr. David D. Dow Falmouth, Ma.
